next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

The bread sat in Maren's hand like a stone. Two days in the bottom of the pack had turned the crust the color of dry mud, cracked along one side, and the inside was dense and cold, the kind of cold that comes from being carried against a body all night and still not warming. Derry had broken the loaf in half and put the bigger piece in Maren's hand without looking at his face.

They were sitting by a stream maybe half a mile off the road, in a spot where the bank had been cut back by spring flooding and then left alone. Birch roots clawed out of the exposed dirt, pale as tendons. The water was shallow and moved without much purpose, collecting in a flat pool before finding its way through a gap in the rocks and continuing. Late summer light came through the canopy in uneven pieces. It was a good place to stop. It would have been a good place to eat.

Maren brought the bread to his mouth and his throat closed.

Not dramatically — no gagging, no heave. Just a quiet, total refusal. The muscles in his neck went tight and his jaw locked and the bread touched his lips and his body said no. The smell of it — yeast and flour and the faint sourness of age — hit his stomach and his stomach moved in a slow, oily roll and he put the bread back down on his knee.

His hands were still shaking. Not much. A fine tremor, the kind you had to look for, the kind you could hide by pressing your palms flat against your thighs. He'd been doing that since yesterday, pressing his hands into things. The bark of a tree when they'd stopped to piss. The fabric of his bag when they walked....
open_hooks_to_carry: Maren's body fighting without his conscious direction — Aldric's training and Vess's instincts operating while Maren experiences it blind
The disorientation of System-blind combat — no damage numbers, no threat calculations, just sensation and reflex
He kills one hero; the physical specificity of that killing
The aftermath: nausea, shaking hands, horror
Aldric's failed comfort ('a clean kill') and Maren's rage at the framing
prev_tail_excerpt:
...eard the part about children. I've got one too, remember? I'm not walking into that settlement with four hundred gold hanging over your head because you picked one bad hole you couldn't pass."

Maren opened his mouth.

Derry kept going, words tripping over one another now that he'd started. "No, listen. I left Pell once already. I left Lenna. I did that. I'm here. Fine. But I'm not doing this part too. We go in there looking like this, smelling like this, and either they panic or they hold us long enough for somebody with a sword and an interface to notice. Then what? Then I die in a place that doesn't even know my name."

Maren looked back at the hollow. The bank they'd built was already sweating light through the seams.

*He's right,* Vess said. *You go in covered in opened contamination and they stop hearing after the first sentence. This buys them something. Not enough. Something.*

Barely enough. A wall of mud and roots. Until morning if the ground was merciful.

He wanted to say there had to be something else. A better ritual. Better hands. Better life, maybe. The words didn't come. Aldric stayed silent.

Half a mile south, a creek cut under the road through reeds and flat grey stone. Maren knelt in the shallows and scrubbed contamination mud off his fingers, his wrists, the outside of the bowl. The water took the dirt and left the smell. Derry washed downstream without looking at him. When they climbed the far bank and found the road again, Derry let Maren go first and kept a half-step of distance that was no longer accidental.

Nobody spoke after the creek crossing.
state_deltas_required: First bounty hunters arrive — trained, System-equipped heroes against a man who has never fought
Maren becomes a killer; his desire for authenticity collides with the reality that authenticity begins with blood
